metadata.seed: I hate looking at myself in the mirror, every time I do I feel like the ugliest person in the whole world. I feel like nobody will ever love me.

patient.query: I hate looking at myself in the mirror, every time I do I feel like the ugliest person in the whole world. I feel like nobody will ever love me.

automatic_thoughts: I am the ugliest person in the whole world
nobody will ever love me

llm_response.response: It sounds like looking in the mirror brings up a very strong negative image of yourself. What specific thoughts or details come to mind that make you feel you’re the ugliest person? Have there been times when someone else has seen you differently or when you felt a bit more positive about yourself?

patient.query: Yeah, looking in the mirror... it’s tough. I just see all the flaws, like the way my skin looks or how my hair never sits right. It’s like all I can focus on. I guess sometimes when I’m out with friends or someone compliments me, I feel a bit better, but it’s so fleeting. I just can’t shake the feeling that, deep down, I just don’t measure up.

automatic_thoughts: I see all the flaws
my skin looks bad
my hair never sits right
I don’t measure up

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a lot of details that feel negative when you look at yourself. What evidence do you have that these flaws define you, and can you think of moments when you felt you did measure up? Sometimes looking at the bigger picture can help shift how we see ourselves.

patient.query: I know, I know... but it feels so real in the moment. I can list those moments when I felt good, like when I was actually having fun with friends or when I did well at work. But then I just spiral back to those nagging thoughts, like maybe they were just being nice or didn't really mean it. Like I’m just pretending to fit in. It’s hard to hold onto those good feelings when the negative ones are so loud.

automatic_thoughts: maybe they were just being nice
didn't really mean it
I'm just pretending to fit in

llm_response.response: It sounds like those negative thoughts feel very powerful and keep pulling you back. What evidence do you have that the people were just being nice or that you’re merely pretending? How might you look at the situation from a different angle, perhaps considering other reasons they might have behaved that way? What would you say to a close friend who shared a similar experience?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I don’t really have solid proof that they were just being nice... it just feels safer to think that way, you know? I mean, I know they care about me, and maybe they really do see something good in me. But when I feel like this, it's hard to even hear that. If a friend said they felt this way, I’d probably tell them they’re way more than they think they are and that their friends see them for who they are. But

automatic_thoughts: I don’t have solid proof that they were just being nice
It feels safer to think that way
It's hard to even hear that

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re unsure whether the kindness you feel is genuine or just a protective stance. What specific actions or words from them make you lean toward the idea that they’re just being nice? And what would it feel like if you could see evidence that they truly care?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do wonder about that. Sometimes when they compliment me, I think they might just be trying to boost my mood, you know? It feels like they’re just saying what they think I want to hear. But if I could really believe they cared, it’d be... I don’t know, maybe freeing? I mean, feeling like I didn’t have to hide behind this insecurity all the time. It just seems so far from where I am now.

automatic_thoughts: they might just be trying to boost my mood
they're just saying what they think I want to hear
if I could really believe they cared

llm_response.response:
**
What clues do you notice that suggest the compliments might be more about boosting your mood than about genuine care? Can you recall any moments when a compliment felt truly heartfelt, and what was different then? If you could imagine accepting that someone truly cares, how might that shift the way you feel about yourself?

patient.query:
It's tough to pinpoint those clues. I guess sometimes when people compliment me, it feels like they're just trying to fill the silence or avoid talking about something else. And then there are times, like when a close friend tells me they admire my work or something, and it feels different. Maybe it’s the way they say it, like there’s more behind their words. 

Imagining that someone really cares... it’s like a part of me wants to believe it could change how I see myself.

automatic_thoughts: they're just trying to fill the silence
they avoid talking about something else
there's more behind their words
someone really cares

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern in how compliments feel different depending on who gives them. What clues help you tell when a compliment feels like a genuine expression versus just filling silence? How might you recognize the difference when a close friend admires your work? If you could imagine the compliment coming from someone who truly cares, what might that change in how you see yourself?

patient.query:
Yeah, I guess I don’t always notice those clues right away. With close friends, it feels warmer, like they see me for more than just what’s on the surface. It’s in the way they look at me while they say it, or when they share a little about why they appreciate me. 

Imagining someone who truly cares… I think that could make me feel more worthy, like maybe I’m not just a burden or a project to be “fixed.” It’s like,

automatic_thoughts: I feel more worthy
I'm not just a burden
I'm not a project to be fixed

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a genuine warmth when close friends show appreciation, and that seems to boost your sense of worthiness. What evidence do you have that you might still be a burden or a project to be fixed, even when you experience that warmth? This could help us explore whether those thoughts are fully aligned with what’s happening.

patient.query: I don’t know... it’s just a feeling I’ve always had. Like, I’m always worried I’m too much sometimes, especially when I talk about my struggles. I mean, maybe it’s just me projecting my own insecurities onto them. They’ve got their own stuff going on, and I don’t want to weigh them down. But there are moments—like those compliments—where it feels like they genuinely care. It’s just hard to reconcile that with this nagging feeling that

automatic_thoughts: I'm too much
I'm projecting my insecurities
I don't want to weigh them down

llm_response.response: What makes you think that sharing your struggles might be “too much” for others? How do the compliments you receive fit into that picture—do they suggest that people do feel cared for? Could there be other ways to interpret the feeling of being “too much” that might align more closely with the genuine care you notice?

patient.query:
It’s just... I’ve always felt like I have to be careful about what I share. Like I don’t want to be the friend who brings everyone down or makes them uncomfortable with my issues. I guess I’ve seen people get overwhelmed with their own stuff, and I don’t want to be added pressure. 

But then, when I think about those compliments, it does make me wonder if they really do see me for more than just my anxieties. Maybe they appreciate my honesty or vulnerability

automatic_thoughts: I have to be careful about what I share
I don’t want to be the friend who brings everyone down
I don’t want to add pressure
Maybe they appreciate my honesty or vulnerability

llm_response.response: I hear how careful you feel about sharing, and it sounds like you’re weighing the risk of burdening others against the possibility that they value your openness. What kinds of moments have you noticed where sharing something personal actually helped a friend or made a connection stronger? And can you think of any times when you shared something and the other person responded in a supportive or appreciative way?

patient.query:
Yeah, I mean, there have been a few times where being open actually led to better conversations. Like, once I shared about my struggles with how I see myself during a late-night chat with a close friend. Instead of pulling away, she opened up about her own insecurities, and it felt... I don’t know, comforting? It made me realize we’re all kind of in this together. 

But then again, I can’t stop thinking about other times when I felt like I shared

triggers: late-night chat with a close friend
automatic_thoughts: being open leads to better conversations
we're all kind of in this together
I can’t stop thinking about other times when I felt like I shared

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve had both comforting moments and lingering thoughts about other times you shared. What do you think might be different about those other moments? How do you feel when you think back to them?
